comment_text.sn10.6:1.2: “Passages of the teaching” is dhammapadāni.
comment_text.sn10.6:1.3: Piyaṅkara means “endearing”. Here we see clearly the role of the yakkha as deity of childhood.

root_text.sn10.6:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā anuruddho sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn10.6:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā anuruddho rattiyā paccūsasamayaṁ paccuṭṭhāya dhammapadāni bhāsati.
root_text.sn10.6:1.3: Atha kho piyaṅkaramātā yakkhinī puttakaṁ evaṁ tosesi:

translation_text.sn10.6:1.1: At one time Venerable Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn10.6:1.2: Now at that time Venerable Anuruddha rose at the crack of dawn and recited passages of the teaching.
translation_text.sn10.6:1.3: Then the native spirit Piyaṅkara’s Mother soothed her little child, saying:
translation_text.sn10.6:2.2: A mendicant recites passages of the teaching.
translation_text.sn10.6:2.4: we can practice for our welfare.
translation_text.sn10.6:3.1: Let us restrain ourselves from harming living creatures,
translation_text.sn10.6:3.3: We should train ourselves well in ethics,
translation_text.sn10.6:3.4: and hopefully we’ll be freed from the goblin realm.”
